sections.intro: The foundation everything else builds on. Understand these perfectly before touching anything else.

cards.title: What Angular Actually Is
cards.contentHtml:
Angular is a TypeScript-first, component-based platform for building web apps. It's maintained by Google and is a full framework — unlike React (library), Angular ships with routing, forms, HTTP, animations, i18n, testing utilities, and a CLI out of the box.

Angular follows a release cadence of every 6 months (major versions). Each major version is supported for 18 months. The current approach is incremental, non-breaking improvements — not rewrites.

React gives you a kitchen knife and says "build your kitchen." Angular gives you the entire kitchen — oven, fridge, dishwasher, and a recipe book. Less choice, more consistency, especially for large teams.

cards.title: Architecture Overview
cards.contentHtml:
Angular apps are built from these core concepts, in order of importance:

1. Components

A TypeScript class + HTML template + optional CSS. The @Component() decorator tells Angular what selector to use, what template to render, and what styles to apply. Components form a tree — like the DOM itself.

2. Templates

HTML enhanced with Angular syntax: interpolation {{ }}, property binding [ ], event binding ( ), two-way binding [( )], structural directives, and the new control flow (@if, @for, @switch).

3. Services & DI

Services are classes decorated with @Injectable(). They hold reusable logic (API calls, state, business rules). Angular's DI system creates and provides them automatically.

4. Modules (Legacy) → Standalone

NgModule was the original grouping mechanism. Since Angular 14, standalone components are the default. They declare their own imports and don't need a module. New projects should be 100% standalone.

5. Directives

Classes that modify DOM elements. Three types: Component directives (components ARE directives with templates), Structural directives (add/remove DOM: *ngIf, *ngFor), Attribute directives (change behavior: ngClass, ngStyle).

6. Pipes

Transform display values in templates. Built-in: date, currency, percent, uppercase, lowercase, json, async, slice, keyvalue, titlecase.

cards.title: How Angular Bootstraps
cards.contentHtml:
// main.ts — the entry point
import { bootstrapApplication } from '@angular/platform-browser';
import { AppComponent } from './app/app.component';
import { appConfig } from './app/app.config';

bootstrapApplication(AppComponent, appConfig)
  .catch(err => console.error(err));
// app.config.ts — global providers
export const appConfig: ApplicationConfig = {
  providers: [
    provideRouter(routes, withComponentInputBinding()),
    provideHttpClient(withInterceptors([authInterceptor])),
    provideAnimationsAsync(),
  ]
};
When Angular bootstraps: 1) It reads main.ts and calls bootstrapApplication. 2) Creates the root injector with providers from appConfig. 3) Creates the root component (AppComponent). 4) Renders AppComponent's template into the <app-root> element in index.html. 5) Zone.js starts monitoring for async events to trigger change detection.

cards.title: Angular CLI — Every Command You Need
cards.contentHtml:
# Project
ng new my-app --standalone --style=scss --routing --ssr
ng serve --open --port 4200
ng build --configuration=production
ng update @angular/core @angular/cli

# Generate
ng g component features/users/user-card    # component
ng g service core/services/auth            # service
ng g pipe shared/pipes/truncate            # pipe
ng g directive shared/directives/highlight # directive
ng g guard core/guards/auth                # guard
ng g interceptor core/interceptors/jwt     # interceptor
ng g interface models/user                 # interface
ng g enum models/role                      # enum
ng g environments                          # environment files

# Testing & Analysis
ng test                       # unit tests (Karma/Jest)
ng test --code-coverage        # with coverage report
ng e2e                        # end-to-end (Cypress/Playwright)

# Schematics
ng add @angular/material      # add a library
ng add @angular/pwa           # add PWA support

cards.title: Project Structure (Best Practice)
cards.contentHtml:
src/
├── app/
│   ├── core/                 # Singletons: guards, interceptors, global services
│   │   ├── guards/
│   │   ├── interceptors/
│   │   └── services/
│   ├── shared/               # Reusable: components, pipes, directives
│   │   ├── components/
│   │   ├── pipes/
│   │   └── directives/
│   ├── features/             # Feature areas (lazy-loaded)
│   │   ├── users/
│   │   │   ├── user-list.component.ts
│   │   │   ├── user-detail.component.ts
│   │   │   ├── user.service.ts
│   │   │   └── users.routes.ts
│   │   └── dashboard/
│   ├── models/               # Interfaces, types, enums
│   ├── app.component.ts
│   ├── app.config.ts
│   └── app.routes.ts
├── assets/
├── environments/
└── main.ts
Core = things that exist once (auth service, JWT interceptor). Shared = reusable pieces imported by features (button component, truncate pipe). Features = business domains with their own routes, components, and services.

cards.title: Lifecycle Hooks — Complete Reference
cards.contentHtml:
Called in this exact order:

export class MyComponent implements
  OnChanges, OnInit, DoCheck,
  AfterContentInit, AfterContentChecked,
  AfterViewInit, AfterViewChecked,
  OnDestroy {

  // 1. CONSTRUCTOR — DI only. No logic here.

  // 2. ngOnChanges — @Input() changed (called BEFORE ngOnInit)
  //    Receives SimpleChanges with previous/current values
  ngOnChanges(c: SimpleChanges) {}

  // 3. ngOnInit — Component initialized. Inputs are ready.
  //    API calls, subscriptions, one-time setup go here.
  ngOnInit() {}

  // 4. ngDoCheck — Custom change detection logic
  //    Runs on EVERY CD cycle. Very expensive. Avoid if possible.
  ngDoCheck() {}

  // 5. ngAfterContentInit — ng-content projected content initialized
  //    @ContentChild is available here. Runs ONCE.
  ngAfterContentInit() {}

  // 6. ngAfterContentChecked — projected content checked
  //    Runs after every CD cycle.
  ngAfterContentChecked() {}

  // 7. ngAfterViewInit — View (and child views) initialized
  //    @ViewChild is available here. Runs ONCE.
  //    DOM manipulation, chart initialization go here.
  ngAfterViewInit() {}

  // 8. ngAfterViewChecked — View (and child views) checked
  //    Runs after every CD cycle.
  ngAfterViewChecked() {}

  // 9. ngOnDestroy — Component about to be removed
  //    Unsubscribe, remove event listeners, clear timers.
  ngOnDestroy() {}
}
Don't use ngDoCheck, ngAfterContentChecked, or ngAfterViewChecked unless you have a very specific reason. They run on every single change detection cycle and can destroy performance.
ngOnInit = move-in day (set up the apartment). ngAfterViewInit = the furniture is delivered (DOM is ready). ngOnDestroy = moving-out day (cancel the utilities). ngOnChanges = a package arrives (new @Input data).

sections.intro: Every line of Angular starts and ends with components. Here's everything you need to master.

cards.title: Component Anatomy
cards.contentHtml:
@Component({
  selector: 'app-user-card',
  standalone: true,
  imports: [CommonModule, RouterLink],  // declare dependencies
  templateUrl: './user-card.component.html',
  styleUrl: './user-card.component.scss',
  changeDetection: ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush,
  encapsulation: ViewEncapsulation.Emulated,  // default
  host: {
    'class': 'block',
    '[class.active]': 'isActive',
    '(click)': 'onClick($event)',
    'role': 'article',
  }
})

cards.title: View Encapsulation — The 3 Modes
cards.contentHtml:
Emulated (Default)

Angular adds unique attributes to elements (like _ngcontent-abc-123) and scopes CSS selectors to those attributes. Styles don't leak out, but global styles can leak in.

ShadowDom

Uses native browser Shadow DOM. Complete isolation — no styles in or out. Not all browsers support all features equally.

None

No encapsulation. Styles become global. Useful for global theme overrides but dangerous if overused.

Stick with Emulated for 99% of components. Use None only for intentionally global styles (like a theme component).

cards.title: Smart vs Dumb Components (Container/Presentational)
cards.contentHtml:
Smart (Container)

✓ Injects services
✓ Manages state
✓ Handles logic
✓ Passes data down via @Input
✓ Listens to child events via @Output
✗ Minimal HTML/CSS

Dumb (Presentational)

✓ Only @Input and @Output
✓ Pure rendering logic
✓ Reusable across features
✓ Easy to test
✓ Always OnPush
✗ No service injection

cards.title: Content Projection — 3 Levels
cards.contentHtml:
1. Single Slot

<!-- wrapper.component.html -->
<div class="wrapper">
  <ng-content />
</div>

2. Multi-Slot (Named)

<div class="card">
  <header><ng-content select="[card-header]" /></header>
  <main><ng-content /></main>  <!-- default slot -->
  <footer><ng-content select="[card-footer]" /></footer>
</div>

<!-- Usage -->
<app-card>
  <h2 card-header>Title</h2>
  <p>Body content (default slot)</p>
  <button card-footer>Save</button>
</app-card>

3. Conditional Projection (ngTemplateOutlet)

<!-- parent provides a template -->
<app-table [rowTemplate]="customRow">
</app-table>
<ng-template #customRow let-item>
  <td>{{ item.name }}</td>
</ng-template>

<!-- table.component.ts -->
@Input() rowTemplate!: TemplateRef<any>;

<!-- table.component.html -->
@for (item of data; track item.id) {
  <tr>
    <ng-container *ngTemplateOutlet="rowTemplate; context: { $implicit: item }" />
  </tr>
}

cards.title: ViewChild, ViewChildren, ContentChild, ContentChildren
cards.contentHtml:
// Access elements/components in THIS component's template
@ViewChild('myInput') inputEl!: ElementRef;
@ViewChild(ChildComponent) child!: ChildComponent;
@ViewChildren(ItemComponent) items!: QueryList<ItemComponent>;

// Access projected content (ng-content)
@ContentChild(HeaderDirective) header!: HeaderDirective;
@ContentChildren(TabComponent) tabs!: QueryList<TabComponent>;
@ViewChild is available in ngAfterViewInit, NOT ngOnInit. @ContentChild is available in ngAfterContentInit. Accessing them earlier gives undefined.

cards.title: Host Element Binding
cards.contentHtml:
@Component({
  host: {
    'class': 'card-component',      // static class
    '[class.active]': 'isActive()',  // conditional class
    '[attr.role]': '"button"',      // attribute
    '[style.width.px]': 'width',    // style binding
    '(click)': 'handleClick()',    // event listener
    '(window:resize)': 'onResize()' // global event
  }
})

// Or with decorators (older style)
@HostBinding('class.active') get isActive() { return this.active; }
@HostListener('click', ['$event']) onClick(e: Event) { }

sections.intro: Angular's template syntax is rich and precise. Master every operator and directive.

cards.title: Complete Binding Syntax
cards.contentHtml:
<!-- INTERPOLATION: one-way, component → DOM -->
{{ expression }}
{{ user?.name }}          <!-- safe navigation -->
{{ price | currency:'GBP' }} <!-- with pipe -->
{{ condition ? 'Yes' : 'No' }} <!-- ternary -->

<!-- PROPERTY BINDING: component → element property -->
[src]="imageUrl"
[disabled]="isLoading"
[innerHTML]="htmlContent"  <!-- Angular sanitizes this -->
[attr.aria-label]="label"  <!-- attribute binding -->
[class.active]="isActive"  <!-- single class -->
[class]="classExpression"  <!-- string, array, or object -->
[style.color]="textColor"
[style.width.px]="width"   <!-- with unit -->

<!-- EVENT BINDING: element → component -->
(click)="handler()"
(click)="handler($event)"  <!-- access DOM event -->
(keyup.enter)="submit()"   <!-- key filter -->
(input)="onInput($event)"

<!-- TWO-WAY BINDING -->
[(ngModel)]="username"     <!-- requires FormsModule -->
[(value)]="name"           <!-- any @Input/@Output pair -->

<!-- TEMPLATE REFERENCE VARIABLE -->
<input #nameInput>
<button (click)="greet(nameInput.value)">Hi</button>

cards.title: New Control Flow (Angular 17+)
cards.contentHtml:
<!-- @if / @else if / @else -->
@if (user.role === 'admin') {
  <admin-panel />
} @else if (user.role === 'editor') {
  <editor-view />
} @else {
  <guest-view />
}

<!-- @for with REQUIRED track -->
@for (item of items; track item.id; let i = $index, last = $last) {
  <div [class.last]="last">{{ i + 1 }}. {{ item.name }}</div>
} @empty {
  <p>No items found.</p>
}

<!-- @switch -->
@switch (status) {
  @case ('loading') { <spinner /> }
  @case ('error') { <error-msg /> }
  @case ('success') { <data-view /> }
  @default { <p>Unknown state</p> }
}

<!-- Available variables in @for -->
// $index, $first, $last, $even, $odd, $count
The track expression in @for is required (unlike the old trackBy which was optional). Always track by a unique identifier, NOT by $index — using index defeats the purpose of efficient DOM reconciliation.

cards.title: ng-template, ng-container, ng-content
cards.contentHtml:
<!-- ng-template: invisible template block, doesn't render by itself -->
<ng-template #loading>
  <p>Loading...</p>
</ng-template>

<!-- ng-container: grouping without adding DOM elements -->
<ng-container *ngIf="user">
  <h2>{{ user.name }}</h2>
  <p>{{ user.email }}</p>
</ng-container>

<!-- ng-content: content projection slot -->
<ng-content select=".header" />
ng-template = a blueprint that's stored but not built until you reference it. ng-container = an invisible wrapper (like a <Fragment> in React). ng-content = a mail slot for parent content.

cards.title: Built-in Attribute Directives
cards.contentHtml:
<!-- ngClass: apply classes conditionally -->
<div [ngClass]="{ 'active': isActive, 'disabled': isDisabled }">
<div [ngClass]="['class1', 'class2']">
<div [ngClass]="classString">

<!-- ngStyle: apply inline styles -->
<div [ngStyle]="{ 'color': textColor, 'font-size.px': size }">

<!-- ngSwitch -->
<div [ngSwitch]="role">
  <p *ngSwitchCase="'admin'">Admin</p>
  <p *ngSwitchCase="'user'">User</p>
  <p *ngSwitchDefault>Guest</p>
</div>

sections.intro: Dependency Injection is Angular's secret architecture weapon. Interviewers love asking about the injector hierarchy.

cards.title: The Injector Hierarchy
cards.contentHtml:
Angular has two parallel injector trees:

1. Environment Injector (Module Injector) Tree

Root → Platform → Lazy-loaded route injectors. Services with providedIn: 'root' live here.

2. Element Injector Tree

Mirrors the component tree. When a component has providers: [MyService], it creates a new instance for that component and its descendants.

Resolution Order

When you inject(Service), Angular walks UP the element injector tree first, then UP the environment injector tree. First match wins.

// RESOLUTION ORDER:
// 1. This component's element injector
// 2. Parent component's element injector
// 3. ... up to root component
// 4. Root environment injector
// 5. Platform injector
// 6. NullInjector → throws error (unless @Optional)

cards.title: Provider Types
cards.contentHtml:
// useClass — provide a class (default)
{ provide: LoggerService, useClass: BetterLoggerService }

// useValue — provide a static value
{ provide: API_URL, useValue: 'https://api.example.com' }

// useFactory — provide via factory function
{
  provide: DataService,
  useFactory: (http: HttpClient, config: ConfigService) =>
    config.useMock ? new MockDataService() : new RealDataService(http),
  deps: [HttpClient, ConfigService]
}

// useExisting — alias one token to another
{ provide: AbstractLogger, useExisting: ConsoleLoggerService }

// InjectionToken for non-class values
export const API_URL = new InjectionToken<string>('API URL');
export const IS_PROD = new InjectionToken<boolean>('IS_PROD', {
  providedIn: 'root',
  factory: () => !isDevMode()
});

cards.title: Resolution Modifiers
cards.contentHtml:
// @Self() — only look at THIS component's injector
private svc = inject(MyService, { self: true });

// @SkipSelf() — skip this component, start at parent
private svc = inject(MyService, { skipSelf: true });

// @Optional() — return null instead of throwing
private svc = inject(MyService, { optional: true });

// @Host() — stop at host component boundary
private svc = inject(MyService, { host: true });
@Self = "check MY pockets only." @SkipSelf = "ask my parent, not me." @Optional = "it's fine if nobody has it." @Host = "ask up to my landlord, but not beyond."

cards.title: inject() vs Constructor Injection
cards.contentHtml:
// OLD: constructor injection
constructor(
  private auth: AuthService,
  private router: Router,
  @Optional() private logger: LoggerService
) {}

// MODERN: inject() function
private auth = inject(AuthService);
private router = inject(Router);
private logger = inject(LoggerService, { optional: true });

// inject() also works in:
// - Functional guards, resolvers, interceptors
// - Factory functions
// - InjectionToken factory

// Composable pattern with inject():
function injectAuth() {
  const auth = inject(AuthService);
  const router = inject(Router);
  return {
    isLoggedIn: auth.isLoggedIn,
    logout: () => { auth.logout(); router.navigate(['/login']); }
  };
}

sections.intro: Services are where your business logic and data access live. HTTP is how you talk to servers.

cards.title: Service Patterns
cards.contentHtml:
@Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' })
export class UserService {
  private http = inject(HttpClient);
  private apiUrl = inject(API_URL);

  getAll(params?: { page: number; search?: string }): Observable<PaginatedResponse<User>> {
    return this.http.get<PaginatedResponse<User>>(
      `${this.apiUrl}/users`,
      { params: { ...params } }
    );
  }

  getById(id: string): Observable<User> {
    return this.http.get<User>(`${this.apiUrl}/users/${id}`);
  }

  create(dto: CreateUserDto): Observable<User> {
    return this.http.post<User>(`${this.apiUrl}/users`, dto);
  }

  update(id: string, dto: Partial<User>): Observable<User> {
    return this.http.patch<User>(`${this.apiUrl}/users/${id}`, dto);
  }

  delete(id: string): Observable<void> {
    return this.http.delete<void>(`${this.apiUrl}/users/${id}`);
  }

  upload(file: File): Observable<HttpEvent<UploadResponse>> {
    const formData = new FormData();
    formData.append('file', file);
    return this.http.post<UploadResponse>(
      `${this.apiUrl}/upload`, formData,
      { reportProgress: true, observe: 'events' }
    );
  }
}

cards.title: Interceptors — Complete Patterns
cards.contentHtml:
Auth Interceptor

export const authInterceptor: HttpInterceptorFn = (req, next) => {
  const token = inject(AuthService).getToken();
  if (token) {
    req = req.clone({ setHeaders: { Authorization: `Bearer ${token}` } });
  }
  return next(req);
};

Error Interceptor with Retry

export const errorInterceptor: HttpInterceptorFn = (req, next) => {
  const router = inject(Router);
  return next(req).pipe(
    retry({ count: 2, delay: 1000 }),
    catchError((err: HttpErrorResponse) => {
      if (err.status === 401) router.navigate(['/login']);
      if (err.status === 403) router.navigate(['/forbidden']);
      return throwError(() => err);
    })
  );
};

Loading Interceptor

export const loadingInterceptor: HttpInterceptorFn = (req, next) => {
  const loading = inject(LoadingService);
  loading.show();
  return next(req).pipe(finalize(() => loading.hide()));
};

Caching Interceptor

const cache = new Map<string, HttpResponse<any>>();

export const cacheInterceptor: HttpInterceptorFn = (req, next) => {
  if (req.method !== 'GET') return next(req);
  const cached = cache.get(req.urlWithParams);
  if (cached) return of(cached.clone());
  return next(req).pipe(
    tap(event => {
      if (event instanceof HttpResponse) cache.set(req.urlWithParams, event.clone());
    })
  );
};

sections.intro: RxJS is the backbone of Angular's async layer. Here's every operator and pattern you need.

cards.title: Observable Fundamentals
cards.contentHtml:
// Creating Observables
import { of, from, interval, timer, fromEvent, Subject, BehaviorSubject,
  ReplaySubject, AsyncSubject, combineLatest, merge, forkJoin, concat } from 'rxjs';

of(1, 2, 3)                        // emits 1, 2, 3 then completes
from([1, 2, 3])                     // same from array
from(fetch('/api'))                 // from Promise
interval(1000)                      // emits 0, 1, 2... every second
timer(3000)                         // emits once after 3 seconds
timer(0, 1000)                      // like interval but starts immediately
fromEvent(button, 'click')          // from DOM events
EMPTY                                // completes immediately, no values
NEVER                                // never emits, never completes
throwError(() => new Error('!'))   // error immediately

cards.title: Subjects — 4 Types
cards.contentHtml:
// Subject: no initial value, late subscribers miss past emissions
const s = new Subject<string>();

// BehaviorSubject: has a current value, new subscribers get latest immediately
const bs = new BehaviorSubject<string>('initial');
bs.getValue(); // synchronous access to current value

// ReplaySubject: replays N past emissions to new subscribers
const rs = new ReplaySubject<string>(3); // buffer last 3

// AsyncSubject: only emits the LAST value, and only when completed
const as = new AsyncSubject<string>();
Subject = live TV broadcast (miss it, it's gone). BehaviorSubject = TV with DVR showing current channel. ReplaySubject = DVR that recorded the last N shows. AsyncSubject = a movie that only shows the ending.

cards.title: Every Operator You Need
cards.contentHtml:
Transformation

map(x => x * 2)                  // transform each value
switchMap(x => api.get(x))        // cancel previous, use latest
mergeMap(x => api.get(x))         // run all in parallel
concatMap(x => api.get(x))        // run sequentially, preserve order
exhaustMap(x => api.get(x))       // ignore new until current completes
scan((acc, val) => acc + val, 0)  // running total (like reduce)
pairwise()                        // emit [previous, current] pairs
toArray()                         // collect all into array on complete

Filtering

filter(x => x > 5)               // only pass matching values
distinctUntilChanged()            // skip consecutive duplicates
debounceTime(300)                 // wait 300ms of silence
throttleTime(1000)               // max 1 emission per second
take(5)                           // take first 5 then complete
takeUntil(destroy$)               // complete when signal fires
takeWhile(x => x < 10)           // take while condition is true
skip(3)                           // skip first 3 emissions
first()                           // take first then complete
last()                            // take last on complete

Combination

combineLatest([a$, b$])           // emit when ANY changes (needs all to emit once)
merge(a$, b$)                     // interleave emissions from multiple sources
forkJoin([a$, b$])                // wait for ALL to complete, emit last values
concat(a$, b$)                    // subscribe to b$ after a$ completes
withLatestFrom(other$)            // grab latest from other when source emits
startWith('initial')              // prepend a value before stream starts

Error Handling

catchError(err => of(fallback))   // catch and replace with fallback
retry({ count: 3, delay: 1000 })  // retry N times with delay
finalize(() => cleanup())         // run on complete OR error (like finally)

Utility

tap(x => console.log(x))          // side effect without changing stream
delay(1000)                       // delay emissions by 1 second
shareReplay({ bufferSize: 1, refCount: true }) // multicast + cache

cards.title: Real-World Pattern: Search Typeahead
cards.contentHtml:
searchResults$ = this.searchControl.valueChanges.pipe(
  debounceTime(300),            // wait for user to stop typing
  distinctUntilChanged(),       // skip if same value
  filter(term => term.length >= 2),  // minimum characters
  switchMap(term =>              // cancel previous request
    this.searchService.search(term).pipe(
      catchError(() => of([]))   // handle errors gracefully
    )
  )
);
This is the #1 most asked RxJS pattern in interviews. Memorize the order: debounceTime → distinctUntilChanged → filter → switchMap → catchError.

cards.title: Unsubscription Strategies (ranked best to worst)
cards.contentHtml:
// 1. async pipe (BEST — automatic, no manual cleanup)
{{ data$ | async }}

// 2. takeUntilDestroyed() — Angular 16+
data$ = this.http.get('/api').pipe(
  takeUntilDestroyed()  // auto-complete on destroy
);

// 3. DestroyRef
private destroyRef = inject(DestroyRef);
ngOnInit() {
  this.obs$.pipe(takeUntilDestroyed(this.destroyRef)).subscribe();
}

// 4. takeUntil with Subject (classic pattern)
private destroy$ = new Subject<void>();
ngOnDestroy() { this.destroy$.next(); this.destroy$.complete(); }

// 5. Manual unsubscribe (AVOID unless necessary)
private sub!: Subscription;
ngOnDestroy() { this.sub?.unsubscribe(); }

sections.intro: Angular's router is the most powerful of any framework. Here's every feature.

cards.title: Complete Route Configuration
cards.contentHtml:
export const routes: Routes = [
  // Basic
  { path: '', component: HomeComponent },
  { path: 'about', component: AboutComponent, title: 'About Us' },

  // Parameterized
  { path: 'users/:id', component: UserDetailComponent },
  { path: 'users/:id/posts/:postId', component: PostComponent },

  // Lazy loading
  { path: 'admin', loadComponent: () => import('./admin.component').then(m => m.AdminComponent) },
  { path: 'shop', loadChildren: () => import('./shop.routes').then(m => m.shopRoutes) },

  // Guards
  { path: 'dashboard', component: DashComponent, canActivate: [authGuard] },

  // Resolver
  { path: 'users/:id', component: UserComponent, resolve: { user: userResolver } },

  // Nested routes (children)
  {
    path: 'settings',
    component: SettingsLayoutComponent,
    children: [
      { path: '', redirectTo: 'profile', pathMatch: 'full' },
      { path: 'profile', component: ProfileComponent },
      { path: 'security', component: SecurityComponent },
    ]
  },

  // Redirect
  { path: 'home', redirectTo: '', pathMatch: 'full' },

  // Wildcard (must be last)
  { path: '**', component: NotFoundComponent },
];

cards.title: Route Guards (Functional Style)
cards.contentHtml:
// canActivate — can the user enter?
export const authGuard: CanActivateFn = () => {
  return inject(AuthService).isLoggedIn()
    ? true
    : inject(Router).createUrlTree(['/login']);
};

// canDeactivate — can the user leave? (unsaved changes)
export const unsavedGuard: CanDeactivateFn<HasUnsavedChanges> = (component) => {
  return component.hasUnsavedChanges()
    ? confirm('Discard changes?')
    : true;
};

// canMatch — should this route even match?
export const adminMatch: CanMatchFn = () => {
  return inject(AuthService).hasRole('admin');
};

cards.title: Reading Route Data
cards.contentHtml:
export class UserComponent {
  private route = inject(ActivatedRoute);

  // Snapshot (one-time read)
  id = this.route.snapshot.params['id'];
  query = this.route.snapshot.queryParams['search'];
  data = this.route.snapshot.data['user'];

  // Reactive (updates when params change)
  id$ = this.route.params.pipe(map(p => p['id']));

  // With withComponentInputBinding() — cleanest!
  // Route params become @Input() automatically
  id = input<string>();  // maps to :id in route
}
withComponentInputBinding() in your router config lets route params, query params, and resolved data automatically bind to component inputs with matching names. This is the cleanest approach.

sections.intro: Forms are where Angular truly outshines other frameworks. Two approaches, each with its strengths.

cards.title: Reactive Forms — Complete Reference
cards.contentHtml:
export class RegisterComponent {
  private fb = inject(FormBuilder);

  form = this.fb.group({
    // [initialValue, syncValidators, asyncValidators]
    name: ['', [Validators.required, Validators.minLength(2)]],
    email: ['', [Validators.required, Validators.email]],
    password: ['', [Validators.required, Validators.minLength(8),
                    Validators.pattern(/(?=.*[A-Z])(?=.*\d)/)]],
    confirmPassword: [''],
    age: [null, [Validators.min(18), Validators.max(120)]],
    role: ['user'],
    agreeToTerms: [false, Validators.requiredTrue],
    addresses: this.fb.array([]),
  }, {
    validators: [this.passwordMatchValidator],
    updateOn: 'blur'  // validate on blur instead of every keystroke
  });

  // Typed form — full TypeScript support!
  form = this.fb.nonNullable.group({ ... });

  // Cross-field validator
  passwordMatchValidator(control: AbstractControl): ValidationErrors | null {
    const pass = control.get('password')?.value;
    const confirm = control.get('confirmPassword')?.value;
    return pass === confirm ? null : { passwordMismatch: true };
  }

  // Async validator
  emailTakenValidator(ctrl: AbstractControl): Observable<ValidationErrors | null> {
    return this.userService.checkEmail(ctrl.value).pipe(
      map(taken => taken ? { emailTaken: true } : null),
      debounceTime(300),
      first()
    );
  }

  // Dynamic FormArray
  get addresses() { return this.form.get('addresses') as FormArray; }
  addAddress() {
    this.addresses.push(this.fb.group({ street: [''], city: [''], zip: [''] }));
  }
  removeAddress(i: number) { this.addresses.removeAt(i); }

  // Useful methods
  onSubmit() {
    if (this.form.invalid) { this.form.markAllAsTouched(); return; }
    console.log(this.form.getRawValue()); // includes disabled fields
  }
  reset() { this.form.reset(); }
  patch() { this.form.patchValue({ name: 'New' }); } // partial update
  setAll() { this.form.setValue({ ... }); } // all fields required
}

cards.title: Template Error Display
cards.contentHtml:
<input formControlName="email">
@if (form.get('email')?.hasError('required') && form.get('email')?.touched) {
  <span class="error">Email is required</span>
}
@if (form.get('email')?.hasError('email')) {
  <span class="error">Must be a valid email</span>
}
@if (form.hasError('passwordMismatch')) {
  <span class="error">Passwords don't match</span>
}

sections.intro: Pipes and custom directives are the building blocks of reusable template logic.

cards.title: All Built-in Pipes
cards.contentHtml:
{{ date | date:'longDate' }}           // April 4, 2026
{{ date | date:'dd/MM/yyyy HH:mm' }}   // 04/04/2026 14:30
{{ 42.567 | number:'1.2-2' }}          // 42.57
{{ 0.85 | percent }}                   // 85%
{{ 99.99 | currency:'GBP' }}           // £99.99
{{ 'hello' | uppercase }}              // HELLO
{{ 'HELLO' | lowercase }}              // hello
{{ 'hello world' | titlecase }}        // Hello World
{{ obj | json }}                       // JSON.stringify
{{ text | slice:0:50 }}                // substring
{{ map | keyvalue }}                   // iterate Maps/Objects
{{ obs$ | async }}                     // subscribe to Observable

cards.title: Custom Pipe + Pure vs Impure
cards.contentHtml:
// PURE pipe (default) — only recalculates when input REFERENCE changes
@Pipe({ name: 'truncate', standalone: true, pure: true })
export class TruncatePipe implements PipeTransform {
  transform(value: string, limit = 50, trail = '...'): string {
    return value.length > limit ? value.substring(0, limit) + trail : value;
  }
}

// IMPURE pipe — recalculates on EVERY change detection cycle
// Use only when you need to detect array mutations
@Pipe({ name: 'filterActive', standalone: true, pure: false })
export class FilterActivePipe implements PipeTransform {
  transform(items: User[]): User[] {
    return items.filter(i => i.active);
  }
}
Impure pipes run on every change detection cycle. For arrays, it's better to use a pure pipe + immutable updates (create new array reference) or use signals/computed.

cards.title: Custom Structural Directive
cards.contentHtml:
@Directive({ selector: '[appIfRole]', standalone: true })
export class IfRoleDirective {
  private auth = inject(AuthService);
  private templateRef = inject(TemplateRef<any>);
  private viewContainer = inject(ViewContainerRef);
  private hasView = false;

  @Input() set appIfRole(role: string) {
    if (this.auth.hasRole(role) && !this.hasView) {
      this.viewContainer.createEmbeddedView(this.templateRef);
      this.hasView = true;
    } else if (!this.auth.hasRole(role) && this.hasView) {
      this.viewContainer.clear();
      this.hasView = false;
    }
  }
}

// Usage
<button *appIfRole="'admin'">Delete All</button>

cards.title: Custom Attribute Directive
cards.contentHtml:
@Directive({ selector: '[appHighlight]', standalone: true })
export class HighlightDirective {
  private el = inject(ElementRef);
  @Input() appHighlight = 'yellow';

  @HostListener('mouseenter') onEnter() {
    this.el.nativeElement.style.backgroundColor = this.appHighlight;
  }
  @HostListener('mouseleave') onLeave() {
    this.el.nativeElement.style.backgroundColor = null;
  }
}

// Usage
<p appHighlight="cyan">Hover me!</p>

sections.intro: Signals are Angular's future. They simplify reactivity and will eventually replace Zone.js.

cards.title: Signal Primitives
cards.contentHtml:
import { signal, computed, effect, untracked, linkedSignal } from '@angular/core';

// Writable signal
count = signal(0);
count.set(5);                  // replace
count.update(c => c + 1);      // derive from current

// Computed (read-only, auto-tracks dependencies)
doubled = computed(() => this.count() * 2);
// Angular knows this depends on count(). When count changes, doubled recalculates.

// Effect (side effects when signals change)
logEffect = effect(() => {
  console.log(`Count is now: ${this.count()}`);
  // This runs whenever count() changes
});

// untracked — read a signal WITHOUT creating a dependency
effect(() => {
  const name = this.name();          // tracked
  const age = untracked(this.age);  // NOT tracked
});

// linkedSignal — a writable computed (Angular 19+)
selectedTab = linkedSignal(() => this.tabs()[0]);
// Auto-resets when tabs() changes, but can also be set manually
signal = a whiteboard that anyone can write on. computed = a calculator display that auto-updates from the whiteboard. effect = a bell that rings whenever the whiteboard changes. untracked = peeking at the whiteboard without signing the visitor log.

cards.title: Signal Inputs, Model Inputs & Queries
cards.contentHtml:
// Signal inputs (replacing @Input)
name = input.required<string>();      // required
role = input('user');                    // optional with default
count = input(0, { alias: 'itemCount' }); // aliased
data = input<string | undefined>();     // optional, undefined default

// Transform on input
disabled = input(false, { transform: booleanAttribute });
size = input(0, { transform: numberAttribute });

// Model inputs — two-way binding
checked = model(false);                  // [(checked)]="value"
checked = model.required<boolean>();

// Signal-based outputs
clicked = output<string>();
this.clicked.emit('hello');

// Signal queries (replacing @ViewChild)
myInput = viewChild.required<ElementRef>('myInput');
items = viewChildren(ItemComponent);
header = contentChild(HeaderDirective);
tabs = contentChildren(TabComponent);

cards.title: resource() & rxResource()
cards.contentHtml:
// resource() — Promise-based async data fetching
userResource = resource({
  request: () => ({ id: this.userId() }),
  loader: async ({ request, abortSignal }) => {
    const res = await fetch(`/api/users/${request.id}`, { signal: abortSignal });
    return res.json() as Promise<User>;
  }
});

// In template:
// userResource.value()      → User | undefined
// userResource.isLoading()  → boolean
// userResource.error()      → Error | undefined
// userResource.status()     → 'idle' | 'loading' | 'resolved' | 'error'

// rxResource() — RxJS/Observable version
userResource = rxResource({
  request: () => ({ id: this.userId() }),
  loader: ({ request }) => this.http.get<User>(`/api/users/${request.id}`)
});

sections.intro: Performance optimization separates seniors from juniors. Master change detection and you master Angular.

cards.title: Change Detection — How It Really Works
cards.contentHtml:
Zone.js monkey-patches every async API: setTimeout, Promise.then, addEventListener, XMLHttpRequest, fetch. When any async operation completes, Zone.js notifies Angular, which runs change detection from the root down.

Default Strategy

Angular checks every component in the tree on every detection cycle. It compares previous and current values of all bindings. If different, it updates the DOM.

OnPush Strategy

Angular skips this component unless one of these happens:

  • @Input() reference changes — object identity, not deep comparison
  • Event fires from within — click, keyup, etc. in this component's template
  • Observable emits via async pipe
  • Signal value changes
  • Manual markForCheck() or detectChanges()
Angular's CD walks the component tree top-down. For each component: check bindings → update DOM → recurse into children. With OnPush, entire subtrees are skipped. This is why OnPush + immutable data = huge perf wins on component-heavy apps.

cards.title: Zoneless Angular (Default since v21)
cards.contentHtml:
// Angular 21+: zoneless is the DEFAULT for new projects
// For existing projects migrating:
export const appConfig: ApplicationConfig = {
  providers: [
    provideZonelessChangeDetection(), // stable!
    // ... other providers
  ]
};

Without Zone.js, Angular relies entirely on signals and explicit notifications for change detection. Benefits: ~15KB smaller bundle, cleaner stack traces, no Zone.js quirks, truly fine-grained updates. This is no longer experimental — it's the recommended path forward.

cards.title: Deferrable Views — Component-Level Code Splitting
cards.contentHtml:
<!-- Load when visible on screen -->
@defer (on viewport) {
  <app-heavy-chart [data]="chartData" />
} @placeholder (minimum 100ms) {
  <div class="h-64 bg-gray-800 animate-pulse"></div>
} @loading (after 150ms; minimum 200ms) {
  <app-spinner />
} @error {
  <p>Failed to load chart.</p>
}

<!-- All triggers -->
@defer (on idle)          // requestIdleCallback
@defer (on viewport)      // IntersectionObserver
@defer (on interaction)   // click, focus, etc.
@defer (on hover)         // mouseenter/focusin
@defer (on timer(5s))     // after 5 seconds
@defer (when showChart)   // when boolean is true
@defer (on viewport; prefetch on idle)  // prefetch early!

cards.title: Virtual Scrolling
cards.contentHtml:
<!-- For lists with 1000+ items -->
<cdk-virtual-scroll-viewport itemSize="48" class="h-[500px]">
  <div *cdkVirtualFor="let item of items; trackBy: trackById"
       class="h-12 flex items-center">
    {{ item.name }}
  </div>
</cdk-virtual-scroll-viewport>

Only renders items visible in the viewport (~20 elements) instead of all 10,000. Massive performance win for large lists, tables, and feeds.

cards.title: Performance Checklist
cards.contentHtml: 1. OnPush on every component. 2. track by unique ID in all @for loops. 3. Use async pipe or signals instead of manual subscriptions. 4. Lazy load routes with loadComponent/loadChildren. 5. Use @defer for heavy below-fold components. 6. Virtual scrolling for long lists. 7. Preload lazy chunks with PreloadAllModules. 8. Avoid function calls in templates (use pipes or computed). 9. Use ChangeDetectorRef.detach() for static sub-trees. 10. Analyze bundles with ng build --stats-json.

sections.intro: Untested code is unfinished code. Angular provides robust testing tools out of the box.

cards.title: Unit Testing Components
cards.contentHtml:
describe('UserCardComponent', () => {
  let component: UserCardComponent;
  let fixture: ComponentFixture<UserCardComponent>;

  beforeEach(async () => {
    await TestBed.configureTestingModule({
      imports: [UserCardComponent],
    }).compileComponents();

    fixture = TestBed.createComponent(UserCardComponent);
    component = fixture.componentInstance;
    fixture.componentRef.setInput('user', { id: '1', name: 'Alice' });
    fixture.detectChanges();
  });

  it('should display user name', () => {
    const el = fixture.nativeElement.querySelector('h3');
    expect(el.textContent).toContain('Alice');
  });

  it('should emit delete event', () => {
    jest.spyOn(component.delete, 'emit');
    const btn = fixture.nativeElement.querySelector('button');
    btn.click();
    expect(component.delete.emit).toHaveBeenCalledWith('1');
  });
});

cards.title: Testing Services with HttpClient
cards.contentHtml:
describe('UserService', () => {
  let service: UserService;
  let httpMock: HttpTestingController;

  beforeEach(() => {
    TestBed.configureTestingModule({
      providers: [
        UserService,
        provideHttpClient(),
        provideHttpClientTesting(),
      ]
    });
    service = TestBed.inject(UserService);
    httpMock = TestBed.inject(HttpTestingController);
  });

  afterEach(() => httpMock.verify()); // ensure no unexpected requests

  it('should fetch users', () => {
    const mockUsers = [{ id: '1', name: 'Alice' }];
    service.getAll().subscribe(users => {
      expect(users).toEqual(mockUsers);
    });
    const req = httpMock.expectOne('/api/users');
    expect(req.request.method).toBe('GET');
    req.flush(mockUsers);
  });
});

cards.title: Testing with Signals
cards.contentHtml:
it('should compute total', () => {
  const cart = TestBed.inject(CartService);
  cart.items.set([
    { id: '1', name: 'A', price: 10 },
    { id: '2', name: 'B', price: 20 },
  ]);
  expect(cart.totalPrice()).toBe(30); // computed updates synchronously
});

sections.intro: The advanced topics that make you a true Angular master.

cards.title: Server-Side Rendering & Hydration
cards.contentHtml:
Angular Universal renders your app on the server, sending full HTML to the browser for fast First Contentful Paint and SEO.

Non-Destructive Hydration (Angular 16+)

Instead of destroying server-rendered DOM and recreating it, Angular attaches to existing DOM nodes — preserving them and adding event listeners. No more UI flicker.

Incremental Hydration (Angular 19+)

Combine @defer with SSR. Server renders the HTML, but JavaScript for deferred blocks is only loaded when triggered. Best of both worlds: SEO + fast TTI.

// Enable in app.config.ts
providers: [
  provideClientHydration(
    withEventReplay(),           // replay user events before hydration
    withIncrementalHydration(),   // defer hydration of @defer blocks
  )
]

cards.title: Angular Animations
cards.contentHtml:
import { trigger, transition, style, animate, state, query, stagger } from '@angular/animations';

@Component({
  animations: [
    trigger('fadeSlide', [
      transition(':enter', [
        style({ opacity: 0, transform: 'translateY(-20px)' }),
        animate('300ms ease-out', style({ opacity: 1, transform: 'translateY(0)' }))
      ]),
      transition(':leave', [
        animate('200ms ease-in', style({ opacity: 0 }))
      ])
    ]),
    trigger('listStagger', [
      transition('* => *', [
        query(':enter', [
          style({ opacity: 0, transform: 'translateX(-20px)' }),
          stagger('50ms', animate('300ms ease-out',
            style({ opacity: 1, transform: 'translateX(0)' })))
        ], { optional: true })
      ])
    ])
  ]
})

cards.title: Internationalization (i18n)
cards.contentHtml:
<!-- Mark text for translation -->
<h1 i18n="site header|Main welcome heading">Welcome to our app</h1>
<p i18n="@@introMessage">Hello, {{ name }}</p>

<!-- Pluralization -->
<span i18n>{count, plural,
  =0 {No items}
  =1 {One item}
  other {{{count}} items}
}</span>

# Extract translations
ng extract-i18n --output-path src/locale

cards.title: Security
cards.contentHtml:
XSS Protection

Angular sanitizes all template bindings by default. Interpolation, property binding, and [innerHTML] all have dangerous content stripped. To bypass (for trusted content), use DomSanitizer.bypassSecurityTrustHtml().

CSRF/XSRF

HttpClient automatically reads the XSRF-TOKEN cookie and sends it as X-XSRF-TOKEN header on mutating requests. Configure with withXsrfConfiguration().

Content Security Policy

Angular's AOT compilation avoids eval() and dynamic code execution, making it CSP-friendly. Avoid JIT in production.

cards.title: Design Patterns for Large Apps
cards.contentHtml: Smart/Dumb Components — Separate data from presentation. Facade Pattern — A service sits between components and complex state, simplifying the interface. Repository Pattern — Abstract data access behind interfaces for testability. Adapter Pattern — Transform API responses into internal models at the service layer. Strategy Pattern — Use InjectionTokens to swap implementations at runtime.

sections.intro: Managing complex state across Angular applications using NgRx, NGXS, and Signal Store.

cards.title: NgRx Store (Redux Pattern)
cards.contentHtml:
NgRx is the official state management library for Angular, implementing the Redux pattern with RxJS.

  • Store: Single source of truth. Immutable object tree.
  • Actions: Unique events that express state changes.
  • Reducers: Pure functions that map `(currentState, action) => newState`.
  • Selectors: Pure functions to derive/query slice of state from the store.
  • Effects: Side-effect model to isolate API calls and async logic from components.
Think of NgRx as a Bank. You can't change your balance directly in the database (Store). You have to fill out a deposit slip (Action), hand it to the teller (Reducer), who then calculates the new balance. If you need to verify an out-of-state check first, the manager (Effects) handles the delay before giving the deposit slip back to the teller. 
// 1. Action
export const loadUsers = createAction('[User List] Load Users');
export const loadUsersSuccess = createAction('[User List] Load Success', props<{users: User[]}>());

// 2. Reducer
export const userReducer = createReducer(
  initialState,
  on(loadUsersSuccess, (state, { users }) => ({ ...state, users }))
);

// 3. Selector
export const selectAllUsers = createSelector(selectUserState, (state) => state.users);

// 4. Effect (Service/API side-effects)
loadUsers$ = createEffect(() => this.actions$.pipe(
  ofType(loadUsers),
  exhaustMap(() => this.userService.getAll().pipe(
    map(users => loadUsersSuccess({ users })),
    catchError(() => EMPTY)
  ))
));

cards.title: NgRx SignalStore (Modern State)
cards.contentHtml:
Introduced in Angular 17/18, `SignalStore` provides a reactive, functional state management approach built directly on Angular Signals, replacing RxJS for synchronous reactivity.

  • Fully functional API (`signalStore()`).
  • No boilerplate (actions, reducers, selectors are merged into state features).
  • First-class TypeScript inference with `withState`, `withMethods`, `withComputed`.
import { signalStore, withState, withComputed, withMethods } from '@ngrx/signals';
import { rxMethod } from '@ngrx/signals/rxjs-interop';

export const UserStore = signalStore(
  { providedIn: 'root' },
  withState({ users: [], loading: false }),
  withComputed(({ users }) => ({
    activeUsers: computed(() => users().filter(u => u.isActive))
  })),
  withMethods((store, userService = inject(UserService)) => ({
    loadAll: rxMethod<void>(
      pipe(
        tap(() => patchState(store, { loading: true })),
        switchMap(() => userService.getUsers().pipe(
          tap((users) => patchState(store, { users, loading: false }))
        ))
      )
    )
  }))
);

// In Component:
// store = inject(UserStore);
// store.loadAll();
// this.active = store.activeUsers(); // It's a signal!
Tip: If you are starting an Angular v17+ project, immediately adopt SignalStore for local component state, and optionally use it for global state. It's much lighter than Redux.

sections.intro: Building accessible, high-quality UI components leveraging the Component Dev Kit.

cards.title: Component Dev Kit (CDK)
cards.contentHtml:
The CDK provides behavior primitives without any visual styling. It's the foundation of Angular Material but can be used to build your own custom design systems.

  • Overlay: Used for rendering floating panels safely (dialogs, tooltips, menus).
  • Portal: Dynamically render templates or components anywhere in the DOM.
  • A11y: Accessibility utilities like `FocusTrap`, `LiveAnnouncer`, and `ListKeyManager`.
  • Drag and Drop: Declarative drag/drop interfaces with custom preview and drop zones.
  • Scrolling: Virtual scrolling with `cdk-virtual-scroll-viewport` to render large lists efficiently.
  • Layout: `BreakpointObserver` to evaluate media queries with RxJS.
// Example: Virtual Scrolling for 100k items
import { ScrollingModule } from '@angular/cdk/scrolling';

@Component({
  standalone: true,
  imports: [ScrollingModule],
  template: `
    <cdk-virtual-scroll-viewport itemSize="50" class="viewport">
      <div *cdkVirtualFor="let item of items" class="item">
        {{item}}
      </div>
    </cdk-virtual-scroll-viewport>
  `,
  styles: ['.viewport { height: 400px; width: 100%; }']
})
export class VirtualList {
  items = Array.from({length: 100000}).map((_, i) => `Item #${i}`);
}

cards.title: Angular Material v17+ (M3)
cards.contentHtml:
Angular Material implements Google's Material Design 3 (M3) specification. It provides fully accessible and themeable components.

  • Design Tokens: M3 relies heavily on CSS variables (custom properties), allowing for dynamic theming at runtime without rebuilding Sass.
  • Typography & Density: Fully customizable scales that automatically push down into all components.
  • Key Components: `MatTable` (complex data grids), `MatDialog` (modals via Overlay), `MatFormField` (advanced input wrapper with error states).
Tip: Never use `::ng-deep` to style Material components. Always use the provided CSS variables or design tokens to override styles safely.

sections.intro: Building rich, performant UI motion using Angular's DSL on top of the Web Animations API.

cards.title: Animation DSL API
cards.contentHtml:
Angular provides a domain-specific language (DSL) for defining animations inside component metadata. It uses `BrowserAnimationsModule` behind the scenes.

  • trigger(): Defines an animation name that binds to an element via `[@myTrigger]`.
  • state(): Defines specific styling points (e.g., "open", "closed").
  • transition(): Defines how to animate between states (e.g., `'open => closed'`). Special states include `void` (not attached to DOM) and `*` (any state). Fast syntax: `':enter'` (`void => *`) and `':leave'` (`* => void`).
  • animate(): Specifies duration, delay, and easing (e.g., `animate('200ms ease-out')`).
  • style(): Specifies the CSS properties to animate.
import { trigger, state, style, animate, transition } from '@angular/animations';

@Component({
  template: `<div [@openClose]="isOpen ? 'open' : 'closed'">Toggle Me</div>`,
  animations: [
    trigger('openClose', [
      state('open', style({ height: '200px', opacity: 1, backgroundColor: 'yellow' })),
      state('closed', style({ height: '100px', opacity: 0.5, backgroundColor: 'green' })),
      transition('open => closed', [ animate('1s') ]),
      transition('closed => open', [ animate('0.5s') ]),
    ]),
  ]
})
export class AnimatedComponent { isOpen = true; }

cards.title: Advanced Animation Control
cards.contentHtml:
For more complex orchestrations, Angular animations provide grouping and querying:

  • query(): Finds inner elements to animate them inside the parent's transition.
  • stagger(): Delays the start time of multiple queried items (great for animating lists).
  • group(): Runs multiple animation steps in parallel.
  • sequence(): Runs steps sequentially (default behavior of arrays).
  • animateChild(): Tells Angular to execute child component animations, which would otherwise be blocked by the parent animation.
trigger('listAnimation', [
  transition('* => *', [
    query(':enter', [
      style({ opacity: 0, transform: 'translateY(-15px)' }),
      stagger('50ms', [
        animate('300ms ease-out', style({ opacity: 1, transform: 'none' }))
      ])
    ], { optional: true })
  ])
])

sections.intro: Internationalization and localization strategies in Angular.

cards.title: @angular/localize (Built-in i18n)
cards.contentHtml:
Angular's native approach operates at compile-time (AOT). The compiler generates a completely separate application bundle for each locale. This provides the best performance because there's no runtime translation parsing.

  • i18n attribute: Mark HTML elements for translation: `<h1 i18n="@@homeTitle">Hello`.
  • $localize: Translate strings in TypeScript: `const title = $localize\`:@@homeTitle:Hello\`;`.
  • ng extract-i18n: CLI command that generates an XLIFF/XMB file with all extracted messages.
  • ICU Expressions: Handle pluralization (`{msgCount, plural, =0 {No msgs} one {1 msg} other {# msgs}}`) and alternate selections (`{gender, select, male {He} female {She} other {They}}`).
Think of AOT i18n like publishing a translated book. You print a completely different physical book for English and French. The reader doesn't waste time translating while reading; they just pick up the French book.

cards.title: Runtime Translation (ngx-translate / Transloco)
cards.contentHtml:
If you cannot use compile-time translation (e.g., you need users to switch languages instantly without a page reload, or translations come from a database API), you must use a runtime library like Transloco or ngx-translate.

  • Loads translations asynchronously (JSON files).
  • Uses Pipes to display data: `{{ 'HOME.TITLE' | transloco }}`.
  • Slightly worse performance than AOT because Angular has to watch bindings and evaluate them at runtime.

sections.intro: Advanced compilation toolchain, security mechanisms, and accessibility standards.

cards.title: Esbuild & Application Builder (v17+)
cards.contentHtml:
Modern Angular uses the `@angular-devkit/build-angular:application` builder, driven by Esbuild and Vite.

  • Esbuild: Replaces Webpack for JS/TS compilation and bundling. Written in Go, it is 2x to 4x faster during builds.
  • Vite: Powers the local dev server (`ng serve`), providing rapid HMR (Hot Module Replacement) and optimized dev-time caching.
  • Bundle Optimization: Angular ensures aggressive Tree-Shaking (removing unused code). Standalone components drastically improve tree-shaking by removing the "heavy" `NgModule` closures.

cards.title: Security (DomSanitizer & CSRF)
cards.contentHtml:
Angular is secure by default, treating all injected values as untrusted.

  • Contextual Escaping: Angular recognizes the context of a binding (HTML, Style, Resource URL) and automatically escapes or cleanses the string to prevent XSS (Cross-Site Scripting).
  • DomSanitizer: If you mathematically know a string is safe (e.g., an iframe URL you control), you can bypass security using `sanitizer.bypassSecurityTrustHtml(value)`. Use with extreme caution.
  • CSRF Protection: `HttpClient` has built-in XSRF (CSRF) protection. If the server sends an `XSRF-TOKEN` cookie, Angular automatically attaches it as an `X-XSRF-TOKEN` HTTP header to all mutation requests (POST/PUT/DELETE).

cards.title: Accessibility (A11y)
cards.contentHtml:
Building for all users requires using ARIA attributes and focus management correctly.

  • ARIA in Templates: Use attribute bindings for ARIA state `[attr.aria-expanded]="isOpen"`. Warning: you must use the `attr.` prefix for ARIA attributes because they are HTML attributes, not DOM properties.
  • CDK LiveAnnouncer: A service to announce messages for screen-reader users (perfect for dynamic updates like "Item saved" or "Notification received").
  • CDK FocusTrap: Crucial for modals. Prevents keyboard navigation (Tab key) from leaving the modal while it is open.

sections.intro: Turning Angular apps into installable, offline-capable Progressive Web Apps.

cards.title: @angular/pwa
cards.contentHtml:
Adding `ng add @angular/pwa` instantly configures your Angular app with a Web App Manifest and an optimized Service Worker (`ngsw-worker.js`).

  • ngsw-config.json: The configuration file controlling cache policies.
  • assetGroups: Caches app shell resources (JS, CSS, initial HTML, icons). Can be `installMode: 'prefetch'` (download immediately) or `'lazy'` (download on demand).
  • dataGroups: Caches API requests. Strategies include `performance` (cache-first, network-fallback) and `freshness` (network-first, cache-fallback with timeouts).
  • SwUpdate: Angular service to communicate with the service worker. Checks for updates (`checkForUpdate()`) and tells the SW to activate new versions so the app can prompt the user to refresh.
  • SwPush: Service for subscribing to and receiving Web Push Notifications payload data.
// ngsw-config.json (Data Caching)
"dataGroups": [{
  "name": "api-freshness",
  "urls": ["/api/live-data/**"],
  "cacheConfig": {
    "strategy": "freshness",
    "maxSize": 100,
    "maxAge": "3d",
    "timeout": "5s" // If network takes >5s, serve cached!
  }
}]

sections.intro: Scaling applications across multiple independent teams and codebases.

cards.title: Module Federation (Webpack) / Native Federation (Vite/Esbuild)
cards.contentHtml:
Micro-frontends allow you to split a monolithic Angular app into smaller, independently deployable applications. These applications load each other's compiled code at runtime.

  • Host Application (Shell): The main app that boots up, handles global routing, and loads remote modules.
  • Remote Application: A standalone application that exposes parts of itself (components or routes) to be consumed.
  • Shared Dependencies: Federation allows you to declare dependencies (like `@angular/core` or RxJS) as singletons, so the browser doesn't download the Angular framework 5 times.

Because Angular 17+ moved away from Webpack towards Esbuild/Vite, the standard tool has become Native Federation, which uses browser-native Import Maps rather than Webpack-specific logic.

// Host routing loading a remote Micro-Frontend via Native Federation
export const routes: Routes = [
  {
    path: 'flights',
    loadChildren: () =>
      loadRemoteModule('flights', './Component')
        .then(m => m.FlightsComponent)
  }
];

cards.title: Angular Elements (Web Components)
cards.contentHtml:
Angular Elements allows you to package an Angular component as a standard Custom Element. This is the ultimate "agnostic" micro-frontend strategy.

  • Interoperability: Can be used in React, Vue, or vanilla JS without the host knowing it's Angular.
  • Isolated Styles: Uses Shadow DOM for style encapsulation.
  • Challenge: The initial bundle size is larger because it must include the Angular runtime inside each element (unless using shared vendors).

cards.title: Shared State in Micro-Frontends
cards.contentHtml:
Sharing state between independent apps is the biggest challenge in MFE architecture.

  • Browser Events: Using `Window.dispatchEvent` for loose coupling.
  • Shared Libraries: A shared NgRx/SignalStore library that both apps import (must be configured as a singleton dependency).
  • Local Storage / Cookies: For persistent shared data like auth tokens.

sections.intro: Angular 21 (November 2025) is the current version. Angular 22 is expected May 2026. These updates represent the biggest shift in how Angular works since the framework's rewrite.

cards.title: 🔥 Version Timeline & Status (April 2026)
cards.contentHtml:
v21 — Active Support v20 — LTS until ~Nov 2026 v19 — LTS expires May 2026 v22 — Expected May 2026

Angular 18 and all earlier versions have reached end of life. If you're starting a new project, use Angular 21.

cards.title: Signal Forms (Angular 21 — Experimental)
cards.contentHtml:
The biggest new feature in v21. Signal Forms are a completely reimagined forms API where form state is managed via Signals instead of RxJS Observables. They combine the simplicity of template-driven forms with the power of reactive forms.

import { form, field } from '@angular/forms/signal';

export class RegisterComponent {
  // Define form as a signal-based model
  registerForm = form({
    name: field('', { validators: [Validators.required] }),
    email: field('', { validators: [Validators.required, Validators.email] }),
    addresses: fieldArray([
      form({ street: field(''), city: field('') })
    ])
  });

  // Read values reactively — no .valueChanges subscription needed!
  namePreview = computed(() => this.registerForm.controls.name.value());
  isValid = computed(() => this.registerForm.valid());

  onSubmit() {
    if (this.registerForm.valid()) {
      console.log(this.registerForm.value()); // signal-based!
    }
  }
}

Why Signal Forms Matter

No more valueChanges.pipe(takeUntilDestroyed()) — form state is just signals. Read them like any other signal. No more FormGroup/FormControl distinction confusion. Compound forms (groups) and repeating groups (arrays) are trivially simple. Type-safe by default.

Signal Forms are experimental in v21. They're expected to stabilise in Angular 22. For production today, continue using Reactive Forms, but start learning Signal Forms — they're the future.

cards.title: Zoneless Change Detection — Now the Default
cards.contentHtml:
In Angular 18, zoneless was experimental. In Angular 20.2, it became stable. In Angular 21, zoneless is the default for all new projects.

// Angular 21 — new projects are zoneless by default!
// No more Zone.js. No more monkey-patching.
// Change detection is driven entirely by signals.

// If migrating an existing project, use the schematic:
ng generate @angular/core:zoneless

// Or manually in app.config.ts:
export const appConfig: ApplicationConfig = {
  providers: [
    provideZonelessChangeDetection(), // stable in v21!
    // ...
  ]
};

What This Means In Practice

~15KB smaller bundle (no zone.js). Faster INP (Interaction to Next Paint) — no monkey-patching overhead. Native async/await works perfectly — no more "weird" change detection bugs. Cleaner stack traces — no zone.js frames cluttering your debugger.

If you're migrating from zone-based to zoneless, ensure all your components use OnPush + signals or async pipe. Components relying on Zone.js to detect changes from setTimeout, Promise, or manual mutations will break without signals.
Zone.js was like having a motion sensor in every room of your house that turned on all the lights whenever anyone moved anywhere. Zoneless is like having smart switches — each light only turns on when someone enters THAT specific room.

cards.title: HttpClient Available by Default
cards.contentHtml:
In Angular 21, HttpClient is automatically available at the root level. No more provideHttpClient() boilerplate in app.config.ts for basic usage.

// Before (Angular ≤20): Required manual setup
export const appConfig = {
  providers: [
    provideHttpClient(withInterceptors([...])),
  ]
};

// After (Angular 21): HttpClient is provided by default!
// Just inject and use it. Add interceptors only if needed.
export class UserService {
  private http = inject(HttpClient);
  // Works immediately, no setup required
}

cards.title: Vitest — New Default Test Runner
cards.contentHtml:
Angular 21 stabilises Vitest integration and makes it the default for new apps, replacing Karma/Jasmine.

// Vitest runs zoneless by default — matching your app!
// Supports fake timers, snapshots, and parallelization.

// Migrate existing project:
ng generate @angular/core:karma-to-vitest

// Example test (looks similar but faster)
describe('CartService', () => {
  it('should compute total', () => {
    const cart = TestBed.inject(CartService);
    cart.items.set([{ price: 10 }, { price: 20 }]);
    expect(cart.totalPrice()).toBe(30);
  });
});
Vitest is significantly faster than Karma, especially in large workspaces. It uses the vi object for mocking, supports ES modules natively, and runs in Node without a browser. If you're still on Karma, migrate now.

cards.title: @angular/aria — Accessibility Components
cards.contentHtml:
Angular 21 introduces a new developer preview library: @angular/aria. It provides accessible directives implementing WAI-ARIA patterns out of the box.

// Install
ng add @angular/aria

// Provides 8 UI patterns, 13 components including:
// - Tree view
// - Tabs
// - Accordion
// - Listbox
// - Dialog
// Each with keyboard navigation, focus management,
// ARIA attributes, and screen reader support built-in.

// These are UNSTYLED — designed as foundations for custom
// component libraries, not direct use in apps.

This is crucial for EU compliance — the European Accessibility Act requires digital products to meet accessibility standards.

cards.title: Angular MCP Server & AI Tooling
cards.contentHtml:
Angular 21 integrates AI-assisted development directly into the CLI via the Model Context Protocol (MCP).

// Start the MCP server
ng mcp
ng mcp -E  // include experimental tools

// Available tools:
// - find_examples: curated code examples from Angular team
// - get_best_practices: Angular best practice guide
// - list_projects: lists workspace projects
// - search_documentation: searches official Angular docs
// - ai_tutor: interactive AI learning assistant
// - onpush_zoneless_migration: helps migrate to zoneless
// - modernize: migrate legacy code to modern Angular (experimental)

// Configure in your IDE (e.g., VS Code MCP settings):
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "angular-cli": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["-y", "@angular/cli", "mcp"]
    }
  }
}

cards.title: Angular 20 Recap — What Graduated to Stable
cards.contentHtml:
Angular 20 (May 2025) was the "graduation" release where many experimental features became stable:

  • All signal primitives stable: signal(), computed(), effect(), linkedSignal(), signal-based queries (viewChild(), contentChildren()), and signal inputs (input(), model())
  • Zoneless change detection stable (v20.2)
  • Template HMR (Hot Module Replacement) stable — edit templates and see changes instantly without full reload
  • Route-level render mode stable — configure SSR, SSG, or CSR per route
  • CDK drag-and-drop — mixed orientation support
  • OnPush moving toward default — Angular 22 is expected to make OnPush the default change detection strategy

cards.title: Angular 22 — What's Coming (Expected May 2026)
cards.contentHtml:
Based on the Angular roadmap and community signals:

  • Selectorless components: Import components directly without string selectors — improves refactorability and type safety
  • Stable Signal Forms: Moving from experimental to stable/recommended
  • OnPush as default: New components will use OnPush change detection by default
  • TypeScript 5.9 support
  • Signal debugging UI: Visual inspector for signal dependencies in DevTools
  • Karma-to-Vitest migration tool stable
  • Enhanced AI/MCP tooling — deeper integration with agentic IDEs
The trajectory is clear: Angular is becoming a signals-first, zoneless, AI-assisted framework. Zone.js, NgModules, Karma, and RxJS-heavy patterns are being phased out in favour of signals, standalone components, Vitest, and MCP-powered tooling.

cards.title: Critical Security CVEs (Late 2025)
cards.contentHtml:
Three CVEs were disclosed across the Angular platform — know these for interviews:

  • CVE-2025-59052 (CVSS 7.1): Race condition in SSR pipeline that could leak request-scoped data between concurrent requests
  • CVE-2025-66035 (CVSS 7.7): HttpClient could leak XSRF tokens via protocol-relative URLs
  • CVE-2025-66412 (CVSS High): Stored XSS vulnerability in the template compiler related to SVG and MathML attribute sanitisation

All patched in v19.2+, v20.x, and v21. This is why running EOL versions is dangerous.

sections.intro: 100+ questions organized by difficulty. Click to reveal answers with memorable explanations.

qa.question: What is an Angular module (NgModule)?
qa.answerHtml: An NgModule is a construct that gathers together components, directives, pipes, and services into cohesive blocks of functionality. Note: In modern Angular (v14+), Standalone Components reduce the need for NgModules.

qa.question: Why did Angular shift to Standalone Components?
qa.answerHtml: To reduce boilerplate, simplify the learning curve, and make lazy-loading and tree-shaking more efficient by removing the NgModule abstraction layer.

qa.question: What is a decorator in Angular?
qa.answerHtml: A decorator is a function that adds metadata to a class, its members, or its method arguments. Examples: @Component(), @Injectable(), @Input().

qa.question: What are the main building blocks of Angular?
qa.answerHtml: Components, Templates, Directives, Pipes, Routing, and Services/Dependency Injection.

qa.question: What is string interpolation?
qa.answerHtml: A way to bind a component property to a template, using double curly braces: `{{ propertyName }}`.

qa.question: What is property binding?
qa.answerHtml: Binding a component property to a DOM property using square brackets: `[hidden]="isHidden"`.

qa.question: What is event binding?
qa.answerHtml: Listening to DOM events and executing a component method using parentheses: `(click)="save()"`.

qa.question: What is two-way data binding?
qa.answerHtml: A combination of property and event binding that syncs data between the component and the view. Uses the banana-in-a-box syntax: `[(ngModel)]="name"`.

qa.question: What is the purpose of the AppRoot component?
qa.answerHtml: Usually ``, it is the top-level component that bootstraps the application and holds the router outlet.

qa.question: What is angular.json?
qa.answerHtml: The CLI configuration file containing workspace and project-specific settings for building, serving, test, and linting.

qa.question: What is the package.json file?
qa.answerHtml: An npm/node standard file that defines script commands and lists the libraries (dependencies) that the project needs to run.

qa.question: What is a pipe in Angular?
qa.answerHtml: A function that takes in data as input and transforms it to a desired output format in the template. Example: `{{ date | date:"shortDate" }}`.

qa.question: What is ng-template?
qa.answerHtml: An Angular element for rendering HTML. It is never displayed directly. It is typically used with structural directives (like *ngIf or *ngFor) which instantiate it.

qa.question: What is ng-container?
qa.answerHtml:
A logical container that does not render an extra element in the DOM. Extremely useful when you need to apply a structural directive (*ngIf) without wrapping the content in a

.

qa.question: How do you structure an Angular project?
qa.answerHtml: Typically using a feature-based structure: Core folder (singletons), Shared folder (dumb components/pipes), and Feature folders (smart components and routing).

qa.question: How do you create a component using the CLI?
qa.answerHtml: `ng generate component my-comp` or `ng g c my-comp`.

qa.question: What is an environment file?
qa.answerHtml: Used to store configuration variables targeting specific environments (like dev, staging, prod), swapped automatically during the build process.

qa.question: What is the ngOnInit lifecycle hook?
qa.answerHtml: A lifecycle hook that runs once after the first ngOnChanges. It is the best place to initialize data and make the first API calls.

qa.question: What is the ngOnDestroy hook?
qa.answerHtml: Runs right before the component is destroyed. This is critical for cleaning up things like RxJS subscriptions to prevent memory leaks.

qa.question: What is NgRx?
qa.answerHtml: A reactive State Management library for Angular inspired by Redux, utilizing RxJS heavily.

qa.question: What is an NgRx Store?
qa.answerHtml: A single, immutable data structure that represents the state of your entire application.

qa.question: What is an NgRx Action?
qa.answerHtml: An object describing an event that has happened in the application, dispatched to the store. Created via `createAction`.

qa.question: What is an NgRx Reducer?
qa.answerHtml: A pure function that takes the current state and a dispatched Action, and returns a new updated state object.

qa.question: What is an NgRx Selector?
qa.answerHtml: Pure functions used for obtaining slices of store state. They memorize (cache) previous results for performance.

qa.question: What are NgRx Effects?
qa.answerHtml: A side effect model. They listen for actions dispatched from the store, perform operations (like API calls), and then dispatch new actions.

qa.question: What is the NgRx Entity pattern?
qa.answerHtml: A utility to manage collections of entities (lists of objects). It provides an adapter with CRUD operations and normalizes data into an `{ ids: [], entities: {} }` structure for rapid lookups.

qa.question: What is NgRx SignalStore?
qa.answerHtml: A lightweight, fully functional state management solution built on Angular Signals. It requires no Actions or Reducers boilerplate and is the modern approach for local or global state in v17+.

qa.question: When should you use Global State vs Local State?
qa.answerHtml: Use Global (NgRx Store) for data shared across multiple distant components, user sessions, or cached API results. Use Local (ComponentStore/SignalStore) for data specific to a feature module or complex component.

qa.question: How do you handle loading/error states in NgRx?
qa.answerHtml: Typically by defining three distinct actions: `loadEntities`, `loadEntitiesSuccess`, and `loadEntitiesFailure`. The reducer updates `isLoading` and `error` flags accordingly.

qa.question: What is the facade pattern in Angular component state?
qa.answerHtml: A service that injects the Store and provides selected Observables/Signals and methods to dispatch actions. This hides NgRx complexity from the "dumb" UI components.

qa.question: How does time-travel debugging work in Redux/NgRx?
qa.answerHtml: Since the store state is immutable, every action creates a new snapshot. The Redux DevTools extension allows you to rewind and jump between these immutable state snapshots.

qa.question: What happens if a reducer is not a pure function?
qa.answerHtml: The state could mutate in place, leading to unpredictable bugs and preventing Angular from knowing if the state reference changed (breaking OnPush change detection).

qa.question: What is the difference between switchMap and concatMap inside NgRx Effects?
qa.answerHtml: `switchMap` cancels the previous inner observable if a new action arrives (good for GET requests/search). `concatMap` queues the inner observables in order (good for POST/PUT requests where order matters).

qa.question: How do you test NgRx code?
qa.answerHtml: Reducers and Selectors are tested as pure functions. Effects are tested using `provideMockActions` and jasmine marbles (hot/cold observables). Components are tested by mocking the Store via `provideMockStore`.

qa.question: What is the Angular TestBed?
qa.answerHtml: A powerful unit testing utility that configures and initializes the environment for writing unit tests, creating a dynamic module for the component context.

qa.question: What is describe, beforeAll, beforeEach, it in Jasmine?
qa.answerHtml: `describe` groups tests. `it` defines a specific test. `beforeEach` runs setup code before EVERY test in the block. `beforeAll` runs setup code ONCE for the block.

qa.question: What is MockComponent and why use it?
qa.answerHtml: A dummy version of a child component used to isolate the parent component being tested. It prevents the test from failing due to child dependencies.

qa.question: How do you test services with HTTP dependencies?
qa.answerHtml: By injecting the `HttpTestingController` (via `provideHttpClientTesting()`). You can expect requests, flush them with mock data, and verify no outstanding requests remain.

qa.question: What are Component Harnesses?
qa.answerHtml: A concept from Angular CDK testing. It provides a robust API for interacting with components (like MatSelect) without relying on fragile CSS DOM selectors.

qa.question: What is a Spy in Jasmine/Jest?
qa.answerHtml: A mocked function that tracks information about if and how it was called (arguments, call count) and allows you to fake its return value (`spyOn(obj, "method").and.returnValue()`).

qa.question: How do you test a component with OnPush change detection?
qa.answerHtml: Since `fixture.detectChanges()` might not work if inputs haven't changed, you often have to simulate the event or call `fixture.componentRef.setInput()` in newer Angular versions.

qa.question: What is the difference between fixture.debugElement and fixture.nativeElement?
qa.answerHtml: `nativeElement` provides the raw DOM element. `debugElement` is an Angular wrapper that provides access to the component instance, directives, and custom query predicates (`By.css`).

qa.question: How do you test Angular Signals?
qa.answerHtml: Since signals are synchronous and pull-based, you simply set the signal (`mySignal.set(val)`) and assert the computed value or template output immediately after `detectChanges()`.

qa.question: What is the fakeAsync zone?
qa.answerHtml: It allows you to test asynchronous code (like `setTimeout` or Promises) in a synchronous way, by letting you manually advance time using `tick(ms)` or `flush()`, avoiding flaky asynchronous tests.

qa.question: What is the difference between tick() and flush()?
qa.answerHtml: `tick(ms)` advances the virtual clock by a specific amount of time. `flush()` advances time until the macrotask queue is completely empty.

qa.question: How do you test an Angular Route Guard?
qa.answerHtml: Extract the functional guard or class injectables into the test file, provide any required mock services (like AuthService), call the guard function directly, and check if it returns true, false, or a UrlTree.

qa.question: Why is NO_ERRORS_SCHEMA considered an anti-pattern?
qa.answerHtml: It tells the compiler to ignore unrecognized elements and attributes. While it suppresses errors for missing child components, it also masks genuine typos (e.g., `` instead of ``).

qa.question: How do you test un-exported functions or private component states?
qa.answerHtml: You shouldn't. Tests should verify the public API and observable side effects (DOM output, service calls) rather than brittle internal implementation details.

qa.question: How do you mock the router in tests?
qa.answerHtml: Use `provideRouter([])` with an empty config, or inject a mock object for `Router` via `provide({provide: Router, useValue: mockRouter})` and spy on its `navigate` or `navigateByUrl` methods.

qa.question: What is the Angular Compiler (Ivy)?
qa.answerHtml: Ivy is the rendering engine in modern Angular. It compiles components into highly efficient DOM instructions at build time, significantly reducing bundle size.

qa.question: What are tree-shakable providers (`providedIn: root`)?
qa.answerHtml: By providing a service in `root`, Angular does not need it registered in any module. If the service is never injected anywhere, the bundler can safely drop its code from the final payload.

qa.question: Explain Zone.js and its role in Angular.
qa.answerHtml: Zone.js monkeys-patches standard async browser APIs (setTimeout, XHR, DOM events). When an async task completes, Zone.js tells Angular, which then triggers change detection from top to bottom (unless OnPush).

qa.question: What does "Zone-less Angular" mean?
qa.answerHtml: In Angular 18+, with Signals and the `provideExperimentalZonelessChangeDetection()` API, Angular no longer needs Zone.js. It precisely tracks exactly which DOM nodes need updating via tracking signals.

qa.question: What is hydration vs destratification in SSR?
qa.answerHtml: Hydration is attaching event listeners and state to server-rendered HTML. Angular 16 introduced Non-Destructive Hydration, which no longer destroys the DOM before recreating it.
